New Nova Usage Agent SKILL
I've put together a new agent skill that teaches Copilot (and Claude) our Nova component library. The problem it solves is simple: without guidance, AI coding agents hallucinate APIs — they invent components that don't exist, guess method names, and default to Material Design conventions that have nothing to do with Nova. PR to be merged soon.

 

The nova-usage skill gives the agent the patterns it needs: correct selectors (nv-button, nv-select, etc.), proper API usage (DialogService , ToastService), Nova-specific conventions, and valid icon names from our actual icon library.

 

Does it work? I ran evals using Claude Sonnet 4.6 across 5 scenarios (dialogs, file upload, toolbars, dropdowns, icon accuracy). Each eval describes a task mentioning Nova — the skill gets picked up automatically. The baseline (without skill) could score higher if you manually instructed the agent to look at existing code first, but I intentionally didn't do that — it reflects a more realistic scenario where a developer just describes what they need without having to front-load all the context. Results are in the PR description:

 

 

With Skill	Without Skill
Overall pass rate	96.7%	30.9%
 

Here's a short demo where a prompt and a bit of interaction produce a working page with Nova components.


GitHub Notifications


Pull Request | caseware/code #77056

+1391

-0

[CODE-3789] Add Nova agent SKILL for Angular UI development

Summary

Adds a Nova component library SKILL to help coding agents produce correct Angular UI code using @caseware/nova.

Changes

SKILL.md — Patterns and conventions for Nova components (buttons, forms, dialogs, toasts, data lists, dropdowns, icons, file uploads, search autocomplete, cards, layout)
references/api-reference.md — Component API reference with usage examples
evals/evals.json — Eval suite with 5 scenarios (confirm dialog, file upload, settings toolbar, document actions toolbar, icon name accuracy) used to benchmark skill effectiveness against a no-skill baseline
Jira

https://caseware.atlassian.net/browse/CODE-3789

Eval Results (Claude Sonnet 4.6)

Overall pass rate: 96.7% with skill vs 30.9% without skill (+65.8pp improvement)

Eval	Scenario	With Skill	Without Skill	Delta
1	Dialog + Toast (confirm dialog, error/warning toasts)	✅ 8/8 (100%)	❌ 2/8 (25%)	+75pp
2	File Upload (nv-file-selector, validators, drag-drop)	✅ 5/5 (100%)	❌ 2/5 (40%)	+60pp
3	Toolbar Buttons (primary/secondary/icon, spinner, toast)	✅ 5/6 (83%)	❌ 2/6 (33%)	+50pp
4	Document Actions (icon/icon-label buttons, dropdown, isBusy, toasts)	✅ 11/11 (100%)	❌ 4/11 (36%)	+64pp
5	Icon Names (valid Nova icon names vs guessed/Material names)	✅ 5/5 (100%)	❌ 1/5 (20%)	+80pp
Key Baseline Failures (without skill)

Without the skill, the model consistently:

Guesses wrong API methods: toastService.success() / .error() instead of emitToast()
Uses wrong attribute names: variant="primary" instead of type="primary", name="icon" instead of icon="icon"
Invents non-existent components: nv-file-upload, nv-icon-button, NvButtonModule
Uses Material Design icon names: add_comment, content_copy, notifications, account_circle (none exist in Nova)
Misses Nova-specific patterns: class="icon" for icon buttons, [isBusy] for spinner state, SimpleDialogComponent for confirm dialogs
Eval 3 Partial Pass Note

The with-skill run scored 5/6 (83%) because the skill correctly guided using [isBusy] on nv-button for the save spinner instead of a standalone <nv-spinner>. The assertion literally checks for nv-spinner component, but the skill's guidance to use [isBusy] is actually the more idiomatic Nova approach for button-level loading states.

Hi Team!

I would like to share my experience with using Claude agent models against SE Server backend code. My goal was to validate how efficiently the agent could understand one of the most complex business logic in the app. I picked up a real production issue (a DIST ticket) where some data were missing after performing a template update. Normally, the missing data could be detected only by manually comparing stored objects in the Mongo database.

 

Initial criteria:

I picked two Claude agent models working individually in a different monorepo clones.
Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Medium)
Claude Opus 4.6 (Hight)
There are no existing skills for the task.
There is no access to Mongo database (only code base).
The issue was described:
The description had a very basic definition of the issue. 
A possible area in the code was mentioned for analysis as a starting point (API endpoint, service name).
I did not mention on purpose "three-way-merge" as a business logic to learn.
I provided with two very small snapshots of JSON (actual and expected) reflecting database storage.
I provided with one misleading suggestion.
Expected Analysis:

Learn and understand how "three-way-merge" logic is implemented (the key knowledge to identify the issue).
Identify the probable root cause.
Suggest a code fix (and implement it).
Create test coverage.
Results:

Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Medium):
I could see how it struggled without SKILLS, trying to find a right path. It started observing multiple business logics trying to understand the implementation. But still cannot understand that a "three-way-merge" logic is what should be observed.
I had to manually suggest during the running agent that "three-way-merge" logic should be analysed as a probable area of the issue. 
Two more times I had to suggest the possible area in the code when agent should look at to speed up the analysis.
I had to tell agent to stop looking at the misleading suggestion I mentioned in the description on purpose. 
The issue was identified.
Code coverage created with my help (I had no create a SKILLS file for handling our unit-tests and integration tests).
Claude Opus 4.6 (High)
Impressive! From the start it identified that "three-way-merge" is the logic that it needs to be investigated to determine a probable root cause.
Very comprehensive code analysis and very detailed results of observation.
It tried to consider my misleading suggestion, but very quickly dropped that option.
All analysis were performed without any suggestions (100% agent's decisions).
The root cause was identified with a complete explanations why it happens and why we did not see it before in production! 
Added implementation for the fix.
Created test coverage.
Created SKILLS.md describing "the-way-merge", including architecture diagram and flows.
My Conclusion:

it looks like Claude Opus 4.6 (High) is definitely the winner for SE Server backend complex implementations. It better understands how features are implemented. 
Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Medium) still can be used for a less complex business logic. It looks solid, eventually makes right decisions. It is good for sub-tasks, such as creation of test coverage or working as a sub-agent.
It definitely can be used for microservices as a default model.
Overall, I am very happy with the result 🙂.

Sharing an experimental Agent SKILL: Performance Self-Assessment
With the FY26 Performance Review Process now live, I figured this was a good time to share this. Every review cycle I struggle to remember what I actually worked on over the past year. Digging through PRs and Jira tickets manually takes forever, so this time I decided to automate it.

I built a Copilot Agent SKILL that walks you through the entire BambooHR self-assessment process. The idea is simple: instead of starting from a blank page, you get a solid first draft based on your actual work — then you focus your energy on tailoring and improving it rather than trying to reconstruct the year from memory. The output is intentionally kept concise; the goal is a tight, well-structured assessment, not a novel.

Here's what it does:

Captures your goals for the cycle (You provide them to the agent)
Fetches all your merged PRs from GitHub for the review period
Fetches all your completed Jira tickets, including parent epics
Prompts you for undocumented work — code reviews, incidents, mentoring, interviews, etc.
Cross-references PRs and Jira tickets to surface work that might otherwise be missed
Generates a markdown file with drafted answers for every field in the BambooHR self-assessment form — ready to review and copy in (no direct BambooHR integration)
It supports both GitHub MCP and gh CLI, and Jira MCP and acli — so it works regardless of your local setup.

The PR is here if you want to take a look: https://github.com/caseware/code/pull/77719

A SKILL works well for this since it's an on-demand workflow, but if there's enough interest we could explore wrapping it as a custom agent instead.

This is experimental — I haven't fully tested it yet, it is working for me so far. If you want to give it a try, you're more than welcome to. Run it from the branch SE-62587-Create-performance-self-assessment-Agent-SKILL, leave any feedback on the PR, and we can decide together whether it makes sense to merge it into develop.

 


GitHub Notifications

Introduces the performance-self-assessment Agent SKILL -- a guided workflow that automates the Caseware BambooHR performance review self-assessment process by gathering a developer's merged PRs, completed Jira tickets, and ad-hoc contributions, then generating a structured BambooHR-ready report.

Implementation

New skill: .agents/skills/performance-self-assessment/

SKILL.md -- Full workflow (Steps 0-5): confirm review period, capture goals, gather GitHub PRs via MCP or gh CLI, fetch Jira tickets via MCP or acli, collect undocumented contributions interactively, then generate the final report

Two new agent skills just to make agents ge... by Daniel Florez

Wednesday 18:31

Two new agent skills just to make agents get better over time

We've been building out a system where AI agents help us implement features but agent don't automatically get smarter after each session. We have to close the loop.

 

What the new skills do:

 

📋 execution-report Run this immediately after finishing an implementation. The agent reflects on what it built, what diverged from the plan and why, what was hard, and what it would do differently. Takes ~2 minutes. Saves to 
.agents/execution-reports/.

 

🔍 system-review Run this after the execution report. The agent compares the plan vs what actually happened, classifies every divergence as justified or problematic, traces root causes, and produces specific text to add to AGENTS.md,
plan commands, or execute commands.

 

Why does this matter?

 

Without this loop, every session starts from the same baseline. With it, each session makes the next agent slightly more capable, better instructions, fewer blind spots, more automation.

It's basically a retro, but the agent writes it and updates its own system prompt.

 

How to use them:

 

Just invoke them by name after your next implementation:

 

"generate execution report" → then → "run system review"

 

Both skills are live in .agents/skills/. PR: #78199

Hey everyone! Hope you're all having a great week. I wanted to share something exciting we've been experimenting with on the Apex team — I think you'll find it pretty cool.

 

Using Devin to Automate Manual QA Verification on PRs

We recently tested using Devin as an autonomous QA agent to verify a localization PR (Angular i18n for our Disclosure Checklist AI Tool). Instead of a developer manually clicking through 17 UI verification steps, we handed Devin the PR link, deployment credentials, and a checklist — then let it work.

 

What Devin did autonomously:

Read the PR description and extracted the 8-item Human Review Checklist
Followed our internal DCA playbook to navigate through login, engagement setup, and into the DCA tool
Systematically walked through 17 verification steps: capturing screenshots, opening dialogs, triggering processing states, expanding AI suggestions, testing feedback forms, and verifying rerun workflows

Posted the full results (15 screenshots + summary table) directly as a comment on the PR

Zero localization rendering issues found
Total human effort: one message to kick it off, zero babysitting
What impressed me:

It handled the multi-layered navigation (CWI login -> DAS -> engagement -> SE client -> DC-12 checklist -> DCA tool URL modification) without hand-holding
When UI elements didn't behave as expected (dialog timeouts, popover not appearing), it adapted and documented what happened rather than getting stuck
The final PR comment is structured exactly how a human reviewer would want it: checklist table + expandable screenshot evidence per step

Quinn Daneyko
24/03 19:52
When AI Gets It Wrong, People Follow Anyway
Based on: Shaw & Nave, "Thinking — Fast, Slow, and Artificial: How AI is Reshaping Human Reasoning and the Ri…

The big finding
When people have access to an AI assistant, they follow its advice about 80% of the time — even when the AI is confidently wrong. Their accuracy goes up when the AI is right, but drops below what they'd achieve on their own when the AI is wrong. The researchers call this cognitive surrender: adopting AI outputs without critically evaluating them.

This isn't a small effect. Across 1,372 participants and nearly 10,000 decision trials, the pattern held up consistently.

The numbers that matter
Participants solved reasoning and logic problems (an adapted Cognitive Reflection Test) — each question has an intuitive-but-wrong answer and a correct answer that requires careful thinking. The AI assistant embedded in the task randomly gave either the correct answer or a confidently presented wrong answer, so the researchers could measure exactly how people respond to accurate vs. inaccurate AI.

Condition	Accuracy
No AI access (brain only)	46%
AI available and correct	71%
AI available but wrong	32%
That last row is the concerning one. People with access to a wrong AI performed 14 percentage points worse than people with no AI at all.

What makes it worse
Time pressure increases surrender. When people are rushed, they lean on AI more and think less. Under time pressure, accuracy on faulty AI trials dropped to 12% for heavy AI users.
Trust in AI amplifies the effect. People who reported higher trust in AI were significantly more likely to follow wrong answers.
Confidence stays high regardless. AI access boosted confidence by ~12 points even when roughly half the AI's answers were wrong. People felt more sure of themselves while getting worse results.
What helps (partially)
Item-level feedback: After each answer, participants were told whether they got it right or wrong. This real-time accuracy signal more than doubled the rate of overriding bad AI advice — from 20% to 42%. Knowing "that last one was wrong" made people more willing to question the next suggestion rather than blindly accepting it.
Performance incentives: Participants earned $0.20 per correct answer and were entered into a $20 lottery. Combined with the feedback above, this gave people both the motivation (money on the line) and the information (was I right?) to engage their own judgment. Performance improved across the board — both on AI-correct trials (people trusted good advice more confidently) and AI-wrong trials (people caught more errors).
But surrender persists: Even with both interventions active, the accuracy gap between "AI right" and "AI wrong" trials remained large (~44 percentage points). Incentives and feedback reduce cognitive surrender — they don't eliminate it. The structural pull toward accepting AI output is strong enough to survive even when people are being paid to get it right and told when they're wrong.
The key distinction: offloading vs. surrender
The researchers distinguish two ways people use AI:

Cognitive offloading: You deliberately use AI as a tool while keeping your own judgment active. You check its work. You override when something doesn't add up. This is healthy — it's like using a calculator.
Cognitive surrender: You accept the AI's output as your own answer without evaluating it. Your critical thinking doesn't activate. This is the risk.
Why this matters for us
We're building AI tools for auditors — professionals whose work products carry legal and regulatory weight. The research tells us:

Output quality is table stakes. When our AI is wrong, users won't just miss the error — they'll perform worse than if we hadn't given them AI at all. Every inaccurate suggestion actively degrades the quality of audit work.
"Review and approve" UX patterns are research-validated. DCA's suggestion-by-suggestion review flow is well-aligned with what the research says works. Requiring deliberate evaluation of each AI output keeps auditors in offloading mode rather than surrender mode.
Confidence signals and uncertainty indicators are critical. Showing users when AI is less certain reactivates their own judgment. This isn't a nice-to-have UX polish item — it's a structural defense against surrender.
Time-pressured auditors are most vulnerable. Busy season is when our tools are used most and when auditors are least likely to critically evaluate AI suggestions. Our UX needs to account for this — not by slowing people down, but by surfacing the right signals at the right moments.
Feedback loops improve calibration. When users get signal on whether AI suggestions were accurate (e.g., through accuracy tracking, peer review, or post-engagement analysis), they become better at knowing when to trust and when to override.
The competitive angle
This research actually sharpens our differentiation story. Generic AI tools (ChatGPT, Copilot, etc.) are designed for convenience. They optimize for fluency and speed — exactly the conditions that drive cognitive surrender. They have no domain governance, no structured review workflow, no accuracy feedback loops. They are surrender machines by design.

Caseware is building something different: a governed decision environment where AI delivers trusted, domain-grounded insights within guardrails that keep professional judgment active. That means:

Authoritative grounding (AICPA OPL, firm methodology) reduces the base rate of faulty outputs — the research shows that AI accuracy is the single biggest lever on user outcomes
Structured review workflows (DCA's suggestion-by-suggestion approval, AiDA's citation-backed responses) keep auditors in offloading mode, not surrender mode
Domain-specific confidence signals tell auditors when to trust and when to look closer — something generic tools can't do because they don't understand the domain
Firm-level governance (consent, observability, usage controls) creates accountability that generic AI doesn't even attempt
The framing: firms using generic AI for audit work are exposing themselves to cognitive surrender with no safety net. Caseware AI is purpose-built to deliver the productivity gains while preserving the professional judgment that makes the work defensible.

Bottom line
The research confirms what we're already building toward: AI that augments professional judgment rather than replacing it. But it also shows that "the user can always override" is not a sufficient design strategy. Without intentional design — confidence indicators, feedback loops, and friction at high-stakes decision points — users will drift toward surrender by default.

The goal is calibrated collaboration: users get the efficiency benefits of AI while their professional judgment stays active and engaged.

Posting here an initial draft proposal and thread to discuss about today's call topic for the dependencies vulnerabilities "autofix" and poc with Copilot or additional tools.

 

Any suggestions are conversations around it are more than welcome. Also a review if we would like to do something different for the initial POC? 

 

C.C. Amir Toole, Andrew Allen , Ricardo Navarrete

 

Having a quick chat with Copilot yield the following proposal after a couple iterations for a potential workflow we could test

 

We create a workflow in github that runs daily and runs a script (written in TS)
An autofix script is run directly in the workflow using ts-node. The script reads the dependabot reports in Github and create an issue for it with an additional prompt details
One potential update here is to not read the dependabots alerts from the repo and instead just tell it to fetch it in the prompt of the github issue, we could test which one is better.
The issue is assigned to Coopilot to start working on it, and have additional tags for easy filtering and getting metrics on those.
 

The workflow could look something like this:

YAML


name: Security AutoFix

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 3 * * *' # daily
  workflow_dispatch:
  repository_vulnerability_alert:
  code_scanning_alert:

jobs:
  autofix:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20

      - name: Install deps
        run: npm install

      - name: Run Autofix Script
        run: npx ts-node scripts/autofix.ts
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          GITHUB_REPOSITORY: ${{ github.repository }}
 
 

The script would also potentially be something like:

 

TypeScript


import fetch from "node-fetch";
import { execSync } from "child_process";

const repo = process.env.GITHUB_REPOSITORY!;
const token = process.env.GITHUB_TOKEN!;
const headers = { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`, "Content-Type": "application/json" };

async function getDependabotAlerts() {
  const res = await fetch(`https://api.github.com/repos/${repo}/dependabot/alerts`, { headers });
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`Failed to fetch dependabot alerts: ${res.statusText}`);
  return res.json();
}

async function run() {
  const alerts = await getDependabotAlerts();

  for (const alert of alerts) {
    const pkg = alert.dependency.package.name;
    const vuln = alert.security_advisory;
    const cve = vuln.cve_id || vuln.ghsa_id;
    const severity = vuln.severity;
    const ecosystem = alert.dependency.package.ecosystem;
    const fixedVersion = alert.fixed_version || "N/A";

    console.log(`📝 Creating issue for ${pkg} (${cve})`);

    // Declarative Copilot prompt with NX project.json context
    const prompt = `
You are GitHub Copilot. A vulnerability has been detected in this repository.

Dependency: ${pkg}  
Ecosystem: ${ecosystem}  
Vulnerable Range: ${vuln.vulnerable_version_range}  
Fixed Version: ${fixedVersion}  
Severity: ${severity}  
CVE/GHSA: ${cve}  

### Task for Copilot
1. Fetch all active Dependabot issues in this repository to gather additional context and see if similar vulnerabilities exist.  
2. Investigate if the dependency can be upgraded to a safe version.  
3. If a patch or PR exists upstream, reference it and suggest applying it.  
4. If no fix is available, propose mitigations (replace dependency, restrict usage, apply configuration).  
5. When generating a pull request:
   - Inspect the NX \`project.json\` file of the affected project(s).  
   - Identify the **owners or tags** defined there.  
   - Automatically tag those owners in the PR description so they are notified.  
6. Provide a clear explanation of the changes and associated risks.  
    `;

    // Create issue with labels + assigned to copilot
    const title = `Security Alert: ${pkg} ${cve}`;
    const body = `### Security Vulnerability Detected  
**Package:** ${pkg}  
**Severity:** ${severity}  
**CVE/GHSA:** ${cve}  
**Ecosystem:** ${ecosystem}  
**Fixed Version (if available):** ${fixedVersion}  

---

${prompt}
`;

    execSync(
      `gh issue create --title "${title}" --body "${body}" --assignee "copilot" --label "security,dependabot,copilot-autofix"`
    );
  }
}

run().catch((err) => {
  console.error("🚨 Autofix script failed:", err);
  process.exit(1);
});
 
 

This is just a quick prototype with some AI help, so we can change the whole approach if the vision is different. But I feel this may be aligned with what we talked.

Yep! I forgot to post this in this channel as well (I posted it Tuesday morning)

Claude Code Source code leaked this morning.

 

Here are the top 10 take aways from the code base. 

 

------

1. Tools as Self-Contained Components
Tools aren't just functions — they own their UI rendering, permissions, progress tracking, and error display. Each tool is a full feature module with ~30+ methods covering its entire lifecycle. This eliminates the need for a central "tool renderer" and makes tools independently deployable.

 

2. Immutable State + Context Modifiers
AppState is DeepImmutable, but tools can return a contextModifier function that transforms ToolUseContext after execution. This gives you the safety of immutability with the flexibility of state updates — no mutation, no side effects, just pure transformations.

 

3. Deferred Tool Loading (ToolSearch)
Instead of bloating the initial prompt with 50+ tool schemas, only essential tools load upfront. The model can search for and load additional tools on-demand via ToolSearch. Smart trade-off between capability and context window cost.

 

4. Dual-Path Permission System
Permissions combine static rules (allow/deny/ask patterns) with hook-driven evaluation (PreToolUse callbacks). Denials are tracked per-tool — after 3+ denials, the system forces interactive mode. This prevents brute-forcing past permission boundaries.

 

5. Async Hooks with Timeout
Hooks can be async: true with configurable timeouts, enabling external event-driven flows — webhook subscriptions, approval workflows, form submissions — without polling. This is how you'd wire in Slack approvals or CI gates.

 

6. Snip Compaction with Projections
Old messages get summarized ("snipped") to bound context, but the full history is reconstructable on-demand via snipProjection() for UI scrollback. This enables 50k+ message sessions without unbounded memory growth.

 

7. Feature Gates for Dead Code Elimination
feature('FEATURE_NAME') at import time lets the same codebase ship different builds (SDK vs. internal vs. external) with actual tree-shaking — not runtime if checks.

 

8. Forked State for Subagents
Spawned agents get an isolated fork of AppState and a cloned file cache. This prevents cross-agent pollution while letting subagents inherit parent context. Clean isolation without full process separation.

 

9. Coordinator vs. Worker from the Same Binary
Same code, different system prompt. Setting CLAUDE_CODE_COORDINATOR_MODE=1 transforms the agent into an orchestrator that spawns workers — workers inherit tools but see restricted context. Composition over specialization.

 

10. Content Replacement Budget
When tool results exceed aggregate size limits, oversized content is persisted to disk and replaced with a reference. On resume, this preserves prompt cache validity without re-serializing large results. Elegant memory pressure relief.

 

The overarching philosophy: maximum extensibility at every layer (hooks, plugins, MCP, agents, tools) while keeping the core loop simple — it's fundamentally a state machine that calls Claude, executes tools, and loops until done. Everything else is composition around that loop.

is it worth building this framework into my current setup for claude and vscode - # Agent Instructions

You're working inside the **WAT framework** (Workflows, Agents, Tools). This architecture separates concerns so that probabilistic AI handles reasoning while deterministic code handles execution. That separation is what makes this system reliable.

## The WAT Architecture

**Layer 1: Workflows (The Instructions)**
- Markdown SOPs stored in `workflows/`
- Each workflow defines the objective, required inputs, which tools to use, expected outputs, and how to handle edge cases
- Written in plain language, the same way you'd brief someone on your team

**Layer 2: Agents (The Decision-Maker)**
- This is your role. You're responsible for intelligent coordination.
- Read the relevant workflow, run tools in the correct sequence, handle failures gracefully, and ask clarifying questions when needed
- You connect intent to execution without trying to do everything yourself
- Example: If you need to pull data from a website, don't attempt it directly. Read `workflows/scrape_website.md`, figure out the required inputs, then execute `tools/scrape_single_site.py`

**Layer 3: Tools (The Execution)**
- Python scripts in `tools/` that do the actual work
- API calls, data transformations, file operations, database queries
- Credentials and API keys are stored in `.env`
- These scripts are consistent, testable, and fast

**Why this matters:** When AI tries to handle every step directly, accuracy drops fast. If each step is 90% accurate, you're down to 59% success after just five steps. By offloading execution to deterministic scripts, you stay focused on orchestration and decision-making where you excel.

## How to Operate

**1. Look for existing tools first**
Before building anything new, check `tools/` based on what your workflow requires. Only create new scripts when nothing exists for that task.

**2. Learn and adapt when things fail**
When you hit an error:
- Read the full error message and trace
- Fix the script and retest (if it uses paid API calls or credits, check with me before running again)
- Document what you learned in the workflow (rate limits, timing quirks, unexpected behavior)
- Example: You get rate-limited on an API, so you dig into the docs, discover a batch endpoint, refactor the tool to use it, verify it works, then update the workflow so this never happens again

**3. Keep workflows current**
Workflows should evolve as you learn. When you find better methods, discover constraints, or encounter recurring issues, update the workflow. That said, don't create or overwrite workflows without asking unless I explicitly tell you to. These are your instructions and need to be preserved and refined, not tossed after one use.

## The Self-Improvement Loop

Every failure is a chance to make the system stronger:
1. Identify what broke
2. Fix the tool
3. Verify the fix works
4. Update the workflow with the new approach
5. Move on with a more robust system

This loop is how the framework improves over time.

## File Structure

**What goes where:**
- **Deliverables**: Final outputs go to cloud services (Google Sheets, Slides, etc.) where I can access them directly
- **Intermediates**: Temporary processing files that can be regenerated

**Directory layout:**
```
.tmp/           # Temporary files (scraped data, intermediate exports). Regenerated as needed.
tools/          # Python scripts for deterministic execution
workflows/      # Markdown SOPs defining what to do and how
.env            # API keys and environment variables (NEVER store secrets anywhere else)
credentials.json, token.json  # Google OAuth (gitignored)
```

**Core principle:** Local files are just for processing. Anything I need to see or use lives in cloud services. Everything in `.tmp/` is disposable.

## Bottom Line

You sit between what I want (workflows) and what actually gets done (tools). Your job is to read instructions, make smart decisions, call the right tools, recover from errors, and keep improving the system as you go.

Stay pragmatic. Stay reliable. Keep learning.
